Rana boylii Research Ryan Peek — 2016-Sep

Objectives

Overview

Overview

As freshwater systems and biota are rapidly declining globally; conservation efforts will require assessment of the adaptive capacity of populations to rapid environmental change. Small populations with limited genetic diversity may have reduced adaptive potential and difficulty responding to future environmental change. Identifying these populations is crucial for effective conservation prioritization and management. As part of my PhD, I plan on assessing an imperiled native amphibian, the foothill yellow-legged frog (Rana boylii) at multiple genetic scales using a RADSeq approach (SNPs). Use the tabs above to view a Map and data summary in the Data & Citations pages.

Row

Background

Rana boylii provide a unique link with hydroclimatic change as their breeding phenology is tightly coupled with river hydrology. They have evolved strong breeding associations with changes in flow associated with rain or snowmelt cues in the spring. As one of the only obligate stream-breeding frogs in western North America, the foothill yellow-legged frog (Rana boylii Baird 1854) historically occurred in streams and rivers from Oregon to northern Baja California west of the Sierra-Cascade crest across a range of elevations from sea level to ~1400 m. For my dissertation, I will focus on three main objectives:

  1. Improve conservation strategies for R. boylii by characterizing range-wide genetic structure, identifying “hotspots” of genetic significance, and potential bottlenecks
  2. Determine the effect of river regulation on contemporary effective population sizes and genetic fragmentation
  3. Determine the strength and timing of population declines (genetic bottlenecks) as a way to identify key habitat degradation events

Map

Mapping

Spatial Data

Multiple data sets exist with tissue/swab samples for R. boylii, but all have been done at different times, spatial scales, and with different methods and objectives. This map will hopefully provide a common place for spatial R. boylii data to be displayed.

Row

Map of Rana boylii Sampling Sites

Data & Citations

Samples To-Date

As of 2016-09-02 01:12, I’ve sampled intensively in the Yuba and American Watersheds with a few sampling sites in other watersheds (Tuolumne, Feather, etc). This is a quick tally of samples collected this year.

Citations:

  • Lind, A.J. 2005. Reintroduction of a Declining Amphibian: Determining an Ecologically Feasible Approach for the Foothill Yellow-legged Frog (Rana boylii) Through Analysis of Decline Factors, Genetic Structure, and Habitat Associations. Ph.D. Dissertation, University of California, Davis, California, USA, 169 p

  • Peek, R. 2010. Landscape Genetics of Foothill Yellow-legged Frogs (Rana boylii) in regulated and unregulated rivers: Assessing connectivity and genetic fragmentation. Master’s Thesis, Biology Department. University of San Francisco, CA. pp. 1-69.

  • Yarnell, S.M., R.A. Peek, D.E. Rheinheimer, A.J. Lind and J.H. Viers. 2013. Management of the Spring Snowmelt Recession: An Integrated Analysis of Empirical, Hydrodynamic, and Hydropower Modeling Applications. California Energy Commission, PIER, CEC-500-2011-TBD pp. 63.

# Objectives {data-icon="fa-leaf"}

##  Overview {.sidebar}

### Overview

As freshwater systems and biota are rapidly declining globally; conservation efforts will require assessment of the adaptive capacity of populations to rapid environmental change. Small populations with limited genetic diversity may have reduced adaptive potential and difficulty responding to future environmental change. Identifying these populations is crucial for effective conservation prioritization and management. As part of my PhD, I plan on assessing an imperiled native amphibian, the foothill yellow-legged frog (*Rana boylii*) at multiple genetic scales using a RADSeq approach (SNPs). Use the tabs above to view a [Map] and data summary in the [Data & Citations] pages.

Row {data-height=350}
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Background

*Rana boylii* provide a unique link with hydroclimatic change as their breeding phenology is tightly coupled with river hydrology. They have evolved strong breeding associations with changes in flow associated with rain or snowmelt cues in the spring. As one of the only obligate stream-breeding frogs in western North America, the foothill yellow-legged frog (*Rana boylii* Baird 1854) historically occurred in streams and rivers from Oregon to northern Baja California west of the Sierra-Cascade crest across a range of elevations from sea level to ~1400 m. For my dissertation, I will focus on three main objectives:

>  1. _Improve conservation strategies for *R. boylii* by characterizing range-wide genetic structure, identifying “hotspots” of genetic significance, and potential bottlenecks_
  2. _Determine the effect of river regulation on contemporary effective population sizes and genetic fragmentation_
  3. _Determine the strength and timing of population declines (genetic bottlenecks) as a way to identify key habitat degradation events_

# Map {data-icon="fa-map"}

## Mapping {.sidebar}

### Spatial Data

Multiple data sets exist with tissue/swab samples for *R. boylii*, but all have been done at different times, spatial scales, and with different methods and objectives.  This map will hopefully provide a common place for spatial *R. boylii* data to be displayed.

Row
-------------------------------------

#### Map of *Rana boylii* Sampling Sites

```{r echo=F, warning=F, message=F, fig.width=10, fig.height=6.5}
library(leaflet)
library(rgdal)
library(geojsonio)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)

# Projection
WGS84 <- "+proj=longlat +ellps=WGS84 +datum=WGS84"

# HBS Museum RABO tissues
RbHBS<-as.data.frame(read.csv(".././data/HBS_RABO_tissues_RAP.csv"))
RbHBS<- RbHBS %>% filter(!is.na(lat)) %>% 
  distinct(HBS_ID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  as.data.frame()

# all potential sites
Rb_potentials<-as.data.frame(read.csv(".././data/RAP_Sites_airtable_20160828.csv"))
colnames(Rb_potentials)[4:5]<-c("lat","lon")

# AJ Lind dissertation sites
lind<-read.csv(".././data/Lind_Samples.csv")
colnames(lind)[9:10]<-c("lon","lat")
lind<-as.data.frame(lind)

# Rana boylii range
# poly<- readOGR(".././data/gis/Rb_Potential_Range_CAandOR.shp", 
#                layer="Rb_Potential_Range_CAandOR", verbose = F)
# poly<-spTransform(poly, CRS(WGS84))

# All RABO Points
# rbpts<- readOGR(".././data/gis/Rboylii_All_Records_thru_2010_full_v4.shp", 
#                 layer="Rboylii_All_Records_thru_2010_full_v4", verbose = F)
# rbpts<-spTransform(rbpts, CRS(WGS84))

# HUC 8 regions
h8<- readOGR(".././data/gis/HUC_8_WESTCOAST.shp", layer="HUC_8_WESTCOAST", verbose = F)
h8<-spTransform(h8, CRS(WGS84))

# HUC 8 Spider
h12spider<- readOGR(".././data/gis/HUC12_spider.shp", layer="HUC12_spider", verbose = F)
h12spider<-spTransform(h12spider, CRS(WGS84))

# Make the Leaflet Map
m <- leaflet() %>% addTiles() %>% 
  setView(lng = -120.8, lat = 39, zoom = 8) %>%  # set to Auburn/Colfax, zoom 5 for CA 
  addTiles(group = "OSM") %>%
  addProviderTiles("Stamen.TopOSMFeatures", group = "OSM Features") %>%
  addProviderTiles("Esri.WorldImagery", group = "ESRI Aerial") %>%
  addProviderTiles("Esri.WorldTopoMap", group = "Topo") %>%
  hideGroup("OSM Features") %>% 

  # Peek PhD sites
  addCircleMarkers(data=Rb_potentials, group="Peek PhD Sites",
                   lng= ~lon, lat= ~lat,
                   popup=paste0("Site: ", Rb_potentials$Site, "
", "River: ", 
                                Rb_potentials$River, "
", "Locality_details ",
                                Rb_potentials$Locality_details),
                   stroke=TRUE, weight=0.6,radius=11,
                   fillOpacity = 0.8, color="black",
                   fillColor = "yellow") %>%
  
  # RbHBS sites
  addCircleMarkers(data=RbHBS, group="RABO HBS",
                   #clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions(spiderfyOnMaxZoom = T),
                   lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,
                   popup=paste0("Site: ", RbHBS$Locality,"
","SampleID: ",
                                RbHBS$SampleID, "
","HBS_ID: ",
                                RbHBS$HBS_ID, "
","Details: ",
                                RbHBS$Locality_details, "
","Loc_estim?: ",
                                RbHBS$loc_estimated),
                   stroke=TRUE, weight=0.6,radius=9,
                   fillOpacity = 0.8, color="black",
                   fillColor = "purple") %>%
  #hideGroup("Rb HBS") %>%

  # # all Rb records
  # addCircleMarkers(data=rbpts, group="RABO Records",
  #                  popup=paste0("Locality: ", rbpts@data$Locality, "
 ID= ",
  #                               rbpts@data$RECORDID,
  #                               "
 Year= ", rbpts@data$Year,
  #                               "
 Source= ", rbpts@data$Source),
  #                  radius = ~ifelse(rbpts@data$Year>=2000, 8, 6),
  #                  fillColor= ~ifelse(rbpts@data$Year>=2000, "darkgreen", "gray"),
  #                  stroke=TRUE, weight=0.6, fillOpacity= 0.8, color="black") %>%
  # hideGroup("RABO Records") %>%

  # # add a legend for all RABO points
  # addLegend("bottomright", colors = c("darkgreen", "gray"),
  #           labels=c("Recent (>1999)", "Old (<2000)"),
  #           title = "RABO Records:
 Year Observed",
  #           opacity = 0.7) %>% 
  
  # Lind Phd sites
  addCircleMarkers(data= lind, group = "RABO Lind PhD", 
                   lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,
                   radius=7,
                   stroke=TRUE, weight=0.6, color="red2", 
                   fillOpacity=0.7, fillColor="orange",
                   popup=paste0("Locality: ", lind$Locality, "
", "RecordID: ",
                                lind$RECORDID, "
", "Tot. Numb: ", 
                                lind$TOTAL_NUMB, "
", "Year: ", lind$Year)) %>% 
  hideGroup("RABO Lind PhD") %>% 

  # # Historical Range
  # addPolygons(data = poly, group = "RABO HistRange",weight = 4,stroke = TRUE,
  #             opacity = 0.3, color = "forestgreen", fillColor= "forestgreen") %>%
  # hideGroup("RABO HistRange") %>%
  
  # # HUC8
  # addPolygons(data = h8, group = "HUC8", 
  #             fill=FALSE, weight = 2,stroke = TRUE,
  #             opacity = 0.2, color = "darkblue",
  #             popup=paste0("HUC8: ", h8@data$HUC_8, "
", "Name: ",
  #                          h8@data$HU_8_NAME, "
", "Alt. Name: ",
  #                          h8@data$FIRST_HUC_)) %>%
  # hideGroup("HUC8") %>%

# H12Spider
	addPolylines(data = h12spider, group = "HUC12-Spider",
							fill=FALSE, weight = 2,stroke = TRUE,
							opacity = 0.2, color = "darkblue",
							popup=paste0("H12: ", h12spider@data$HUC_12, "
", "Name: ",
													 h12spider@data$HU_12_NAME, "
", "Acres: ",
													 h12spider@data$ACRES)) %>%
	hideGroup("HUC12-Spider") %>%
	
  # add controls for basemaps and data
  addLayersControl(
    baseGroups = c("OSM", "ESRI Aerial", "Topo"),
    overlayGroups = c("RABO Lind PhD",
                      "RABO HBS","HUC12-Spider",
                      "Peek PhD Sites", "OSM Features"),
    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = T))

m

#saveWidget(widget = m, file="RABO.html", selfcontained = FALSE)

```

# Data & Citations {data-icon="fa-book"}

##  Samples To-Date {.sidebar}

As of `r format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")`, I've sampled intensively in the Yuba and American Watersheds with a few sampling sites in other watersheds (Tuolumne, Feather, etc). This is a quick tally of samples collected this year.

Row {data-height=150}
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Total Samples 

```{r total}
rbsamples<-read.csv(".././data/RAP_Samples_airtable_20160828.csv")
rbtotal<- as.numeric(rbsamples %>% count() + 492)
valueBox(rbtotal, icon = "ion-erlenmeyer-flask")
```

### Adult Females

```{r female}
rbsamples<-read.csv(".././data/RAP_Samples_airtable_20160828.csv")
rbfemales<- as.numeric(rbsamples  %>% filter(Stage=='A' & Sex=='F')  %>% count())
valueBox(rbfemales, icon = "ion-female")
```

### Adult Males

```{r male}
rbmales<- as.numeric(rbsamples %>% filter(Stage=='A' & Sex=='M')  %>% count())
valueBox(rbmales, icon = "ion-male")
```

### Juveniles

```{r juve}
rbjuvs<- as.numeric(rbsamples %>% filter(Stage=='J')  %>% count())
valueBox(rbjuvs, icon = "fa-neuter")
```

### Tadpoles

```{r tads}
rbtads<- as.numeric(rbsamples %>% filter(Stage=='T')  %>% count())
valueBox(rbtads, icon = "ion-waterdrop")
```

Row {data-height=150}
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

### Tissue Samples 

```{r tissue}
rbtissue <- rbsamples %>% 
	filter(!Tissue=="N") %>%
	count() %>% 
	as.numeric + 492
valueBox(rbtissue, icon = "ion-ionic")
```

### Buccal Swabs

```{r bswabs}
rb_buccal<- rbsamples %>% filter(Swab=='B') %>% count() %>% 
	as.numeric
valueBox(rb_buccal, icon = "ion-ios-checkmark")
```

### Skin Swabs

```{r sswabs}
rb_skin<- rbsamples %>% filter(Swab=='B')  %>% count() %>% 
	as.numeric
valueBox(rb_skin, icon = "ion-ios-checkmark-outline")
```

### RASI CDFW buccal

```{r rasi_cdfw}
valueBox(111, icon = "ion-ios-plus")
```

### RASI WSU buccal

```{r rasi_wsu}
valueBox(174, icon = "ion-ios-circle-filled") # plus 7 tail clips
```



Row {data-height=350}
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

#### Table of Data Sources and Citations:  

Data Source     | Data Type     | Sites   | Samples |
| :------------- | :------------- | :------------- | :------------- |
| UCD Peek  | Tissue/Buccal Swabs      |  161  | 335 |
| HBS Lind  | Tissue      | 67   | 492    |
| WSU Bedwell | Buccal Swabs | ? | 174 | 
| CDFW Mussulman | Buccal Swabs | 5-6? | 111 | 


### Citations:  

 - Lind, A.J. 2005. Reintroduction of a Declining Amphibian: Determining an Ecologically Feasible Approach for the Foothill Yellow-legged Frog (*Rana boylii*) Through Analysis of Decline Factors, Genetic Structure, and Habitat Associations. Ph.D. Dissertation, University of California, Davis, California, USA, 169 p
 
 - Peek, R. 2010. Landscape Genetics of Foothill Yellow-legged Frogs (*Rana boylii*) in regulated and unregulated rivers: Assessing connectivity and genetic fragmentation. Master’s Thesis, Biology Department. University of San Francisco, CA.  pp. 1-69.
 
 - Yarnell, S.M., R.A. Peek, D.E. Rheinheimer, A.J. Lind and J.H. Viers. 2013. Management of the Spring Snowmelt Recession: An Integrated Analysis of Empirical, Hydrodynamic, and Hydropower Modeling Applications. California Energy Commission, PIER, CEC-500-2011-TBD pp. 63.
